title: Part I: The House by the Medlar Tree
paragraphs: Once upon a time there was the Malavoglia family—grandfather Toni, his son Bastiano called Bastianazzo, and his grandchildren. They lived in the village of Aci Trezza, on the coast of Sicily, in a house by an old medlar tree that everyone in the village knew as the Malavoglia house.

The name Malavoglia meant, roughly, 'bad will' or 'reluctant'—which was ironic, because the Malavoglias were not lazy. They were fishermen, hardworking and honest, and they had lived in that house by the medlar tree for as long as anyone could remember. Grandfather Toni—they called him Padron Ntoni—was the head of the family. He was a stout old man with a white beard, and he had a proverb for every situation, which his family found comforting and his neighbors found tiresome.

'The world is what it is,' Padron Ntoni would say, 'and it's been what it is for a long time.' And: 'You need five things to make a house: a roof against the rain, a fire against the cold, bread for hunger, wine for thirst, and people who love you.' He had all five, more or less.

There was Bastianazzo—mild, obedient, the kind of man who did what he was told and was glad to do it. There was La Longa, Bastianazzo's wife, a small, dark, industrious woman who worked always with something in her hands. There were the five grandchildren: Ntoni, Luca, Mena, Alessi, and the little one, Lia.

They were not rich. They owned the house by the medlar tree and a boat—called the Provvidenza, Providence—and their nets, and that was all. But they were together, and the sea gave them enough, and the house had a fire and bread and wine and people who loved each other.
Then came the year when everything changed.

The government was building roads and there were men coming through Aci Trezza looking for workers. Young Ntoni—the eldest grandson—was restless. He was nineteen and he had seen other men go away and come back with money and stories.
'I want to go,' he told his grandfather.
'Stay home,' said Padron Ntoni. 'A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.'
'But grandfather—'
'Here you have the house and the boat and your family. Out there you have nothing but what you can find. Those who leave Aci Trezza come back poorer.'
Ntoni did not leave—not yet. But the restlessness stayed in him.

title: Part II: The Debt
paragraphs: The trouble began with a cargo of lupins.

A man named Uncle Crocifisso had a shipment of dried lupins—a kind of bean—that he wanted taken to another town for sale. The Malavoglias agreed to carry the cargo in the Provvidenza. They would share the profit. It was a good opportunity.
Bastianazzo loaded the boat and sailed. He did not return.

There was a storm—the worst storm anyone remembered—and the Provvidenza went down with all hands, including Bastianazzo. The lupins were lost. Bastianazzo was lost. And because the cargo had been loaded on credit—the Malavoglias had not yet paid Uncle Crocifisso for the lupins—there was now a debt.
Uncle Crocifisso was not a cruel man, exactly. He was a practical man. He wanted his money.
Padron Ntoni went to him.
'My son is dead,' he said. 'The boat is lost. Give me time.'
'I am sorry for your son,' said Uncle Crocifisso. 'But business is business.'
They arranged terms. The debt would be paid. The house would serve as surety.

So the Malavoglias had lost Bastianazzo, lost the Provvidenza, and now owed money—money they did not have—with the house as security. La Longa, the widow, sat in the house with her children and her grief and her debts, and Padron Ntoni looked at the sea.
Young Ntoni was drafted into the navy. He went away for three years. He came back a man—or at least taller—but without money, and with new ideas about how people lived in other parts of the world.
'In Napoli,' he said, 'they eat white bread and drink good wine. Here we eat black bread and drink water.'
'Here,' said Padron Ntoni, 'you eat what your family has earned.'
'I want more than that.'
'You want more. But you must work for more. The world doesn't give anything away.'
Ntoni knew this was true. But knowing it was true and accepting it were different things.

title: Part III: The Dispersal
paragraphs: The family worked to pay the debt. They worked for years—fishing, mending nets, taking any work they could find. Mena, the eldest granddaughter, gave up a good marriage because the family's poverty made it impossible. Alessi, the steady, quiet one, worked as hard as anyone.
They repaid the debt. They bought back the Provvidenza—rebuilt her—and began again.
But Ntoni was already lost.

He had tried to settle, tried to work, tried to be what his grandfather needed him to be. But the restlessness had become a habit, and the habit had become something worse. He fell in with bad company, drank too much, worked too little. He was involved—no one quite knew how—in a smuggling operation. There was a customs officer killed. Ntoni did not kill him, but he was there.
He was tried and convicted and sent to prison.

Padron Ntoni went on working, but he was old now and growing frail. The village watched and said nothing or said everything, which in a small village is the same thing. He went to visit Ntoni in prison once. They sat on either side of a screen.
'Are you well?' he said.
'Well enough,' said Ntoni.
They did not have much else to say. Between them was the screen and everything that had gone wrong.

Padron Ntoni died before Ntoni was released. La Longa died too—of grief and work and the damp from the sea. Mena never married. Alessi married a good girl and had children and bought back the house by the medlar tree. The house remained—solid and humble, under its old medlar tree, looking out at the sea.
Ntoni came back to Aci Trezza one evening. He walked through the village and no one spoke to him. He found Alessi.
'I can't stay,' he said. 'I thought I could, but I can't. The village—it looks at me with those eyes. I'll go away again.'
'Where?' said Alessi.
'Somewhere,' said Ntoni. 'Away.'
He looked at the house—the house by the medlar tree—and felt something that he could not name. Then he turned and walked down to the harbor and got on a boat and went away.
Alessi stayed. He had the house and the boat and his family. He was Padron Ntoni's true heir—not the eldest, not the most gifted, but the one who stayed.
The sea went on, indifferent and eternal. The medlar tree stood by the house. The village endured, as villages do, one generation after another, each one carrying the weight of the one before.
